title: Why Version Control?
subtitle: The problem version control solves

sections.content: Imagine writing an essay. You make changes, save. Make more changes. Then realize the version from 2 hours ago was better. But it's gone. Now imagine this with code — hundreds of files, multiple people editing.
sections.analogy: Version control is like a save point in a video game. You can always go back.
keyPoints: You can go back to any previous version
Multiple people can work on the same project

sections.question: What is the main problem version control solves?
options: Tracking changes and being able to undo them
sections.explanation: Version control tracks every change you make to your files. If something goes wrong, you can always go back to a previous version. It doesn't make code faster or fix bugs — it keeps a history of everything.

sections.content: The tool developers use for version control is called **git**. Created by Linus Torvalds (who also created Linux). Nearly every software project in the world uses it.
keyPoints: git tracks changes to files over time
GitHub is a website that hosts git repositories online
A repository (repo) is a project tracked by git

completionMessage: git is the most important tool you'll learn as a developer. Every company, every open-source project, every codebase uses it. Next, you'll run your very first git command.
